title: Artificial Intelligence
school: College of Emergency Preparedness, Homeland Security and Cybersecurity (CEHC)
prereqs: Grade of C or better in AMAT 220 or ICSI 311; AMAT 367 or AMAT 370; ICSI 401 and ICSI 403
description: An introduction to the broad spectrum of approaches and techniques of Artificial Intelligence, such as search and planning problems, basic machine learning, Markov decision processes, reinforcement learning, Bayesian networks, propositional and first-order logic, and potentially introduction to deep learning.

title: Machine Learning
school: College of Emergency Preparedness, Homeland Security and Cybersecurity (CEHC)
description: Introduces the principles and practice of Bayesian and maximum entropy methods for data analysis, signal processing, and machine learning. Hands-on course using MATLAB. Topics include gradient ascent, fixed-point methods, and Markov chain Monte Carlo sampling techniques.

title: Concepts of Artificial Intelligence
school: College of Emergency Preparedness, Homeland Security and Cybersecurity (CEHC)
description: An introduction to the technical foundations of artificial intelligence as well as its relevance to, and implications for modern society. Covers basics of AI and machine learning (AI/ML), data selection and handling, and issues of fairness, bias, and explainability.

title: Ethical Considerations in Artificial Intelligence
school: College of Emergency Preparedness, Homeland Security and Cybersecurity (CEHC)
description: Explores different aspects of ethical and moral implications of the use of artificial intelligence and machine learning systems — from societal consequences to technical issues such as bias, fairness, explainability, and legal implications.

title: Applied Machine Learning
school: College of Emergency Preparedness, Homeland Security and Cybersecurity (CEHC)
description: A technically-oriented course in the concepts and implementation of machine learning systems. Covers unsupervised and supervised approaches, a variety of algorithms, and the critical importance of data. Includes extensive implementation exercises and projects.

title: Data Analytics and AI in Cybersecurity
school: College of Emergency Preparedness, Homeland Security and Cybersecurity (CEHC)
prereqs: CYBR 108 or CINF 108 or ICSI 201, or permission of instructor
description: Equips students with knowledge and skills to analyze and interpret vast amounts of data, enabling proactive detection and mitigation of cyber threats. Covers the fundamentals of data analytics and AI techniques in cybersecurity and the technical background to implement basic AI techniques using Python.

title: Generative AI for Cybersecurity
school: College of Emergency Preparedness, Homeland Security and Cybersecurity (CEHC)
prereqs: CYBR 108 or CINF 108 or ICSI 201, or permission of instructor
description: A comprehensive introduction to generative artificial intelligence and its applications in cybersecurity. Students learn principles behind generative models including neural networks, deep learning, and GANs, and explore their practical use in image generation, text generation, and cyber defense.

title: Trustworthy AI
school: College of Emergency Preparedness, Homeland Security and Cybersecurity (CEHC)
description: Provides a deep understanding of state-of-the-art AI methods designed to make AI more trustworthy against unforeseen faults, adversarial manipulation, and violations of ethical norms in privacy and fairness. Covers methods and tools for deploying transparent, ethically sound, and robust machine learning solutions.

title: Introduction to Machine Learning for Engineers
school: College of Nanotechnology, Science, and Engineering (CNSE)
prereqs: AMAT 214, AMAT 220, IECE 371 and either IECE 233 or IECE 332
description: A comprehensive introduction to machine learning for IoT applications. Covers ML theory, deep learning for communication, and reinforcement learning control. Assignments involve coding-based tasks to design and implement ML models.

title: Disruptive Financial Technology
prereqs: Declared School of Business major; BFIN 236, BFIN 300, and BFIN 301
description: An overview of financial technologies including blockchain and artificial intelligence. Covers how AI works in financial businesses, transformative effects on insurance, lending, crowdfunding, and personal banking, and the balance between regulation and innovation.

title: Introduction to Artificial Intelligence
description: A one-credit, three-to-four week interdisciplinary course covering AI fundamentals, societal implications, and ethical considerations. Taught by AI & Society Dissertation Fellows. No prior technical experience required.

title: Artificial Intelligence I
school: College of Emergency Preparedness, Homeland Security and Cybersecurity (CEHC)
prereqs: C or better in CSI 521 or passing Departmental exam in discrete mathematics. Students with credit for ICSI 435 cannot receive credit.
description: A first course in artificial intelligence introducing basic concepts and techniques. Topics include problem representation, production systems, heuristic search, predicate logic, and structured representation of knowledge.

title: Machine Learning
school: College of Emergency Preparedness, Homeland Security and Cybersecurity (CEHC)
prereqs: C or above in CSI 521 or passing Departmental exam in discrete math. Students with credit for CSI 436 cannot receive credit.
description: A broad introduction to machine learning covering numerical optimization, dimension reduction, classification, regression, clustering, neural networks, and support vector machines. Applied in engineering, bioinformatics, data mining, and neurosciences.

title: Artificial Intelligence II
school: College of Emergency Preparedness, Homeland Security and Cybersecurity (CEHC)
description: Advanced topics in artificial intelligence building on foundational AI concepts. Covers advanced search, planning, knowledge representation, and reasoning systems.

title: Topics in Artificial Intelligence
school: College of Emergency Preparedness, Homeland Security and Cybersecurity (CEHC)
description: Advanced topics in Artificial Intelligence. Topics may vary by semester. May be repeated for credit with permission of the department.

title: Applied Machine Learning
school: College of Emergency Preparedness, Homeland Security and Cybersecurity (CEHC)
description: A technically-oriented course in the concepts and implementation of machine learning systems. Covers unsupervised and supervised approaches, variety of algorithms, and the critical importance of data for machine learning.

title: Machine Learning
description: Provides statistical tools and mathematical principles for traditional and modern data science problems. Covers a wide variety of machine learning topics with emphasis on critical analytical skills, including boosting and online learning for large-scale data analysis.

title: Practical Methods in Machine Learning
description: Capstone for the Machine Learning sequence. Students select a real-world data analysis project, performing data preprocessing, project design, comparison of ML methods, and critical analysis of results with close instructor guidance.

title: Artificial Intelligence with Geospatial Big Data
prereqs: GOG 408 or 508, or a similar introductory course on spatial data science. Basic statistics and GIS knowledge required.
description: Introduces concepts, theories, techniques, and analytical methods of artificial intelligence for geospatial big data. Covers data from remote sensing, historic archives, monitoring programs, and location-based services. Practical applications using advanced Python programming.

title: Caring for Older Adults: Policy, Practice, and Innovation
description: Comprehensive overview of the long-term care continuum examining health policy, organization, financing, and service delivery among older adults. Covers the role of artificial intelligence in enhancing long-term care with a focus on improving patient outcomes, optimizing care delivery, and supporting decision-making processes.

title: AI in the Classroom
prereqs: Students with credit for ETAP 431 cannot receive credit.
description: Provides educators with foundational knowledge and practical skills to leverage AI technologies effectively and ethically in educational settings. Covers AI basics, generative chatbots, and AI applications tailored for the classroom. No coding knowledge required.

title: Generative Artificial Intelligence and Large Language Models
description: Covers generative AI and large language models with applications in business contexts. Students learn to apply LLMs, understand their capabilities and limitations, and explore use cases across business domains.

title: Artificial Intelligence and Strategic Decision Making
description: Explores how AI technologies can be strategically leveraged by organizations for improving business efficiency and profitability. Covers deep learning, large language models, generative AI, ethics and responsible AI, and autonomous systems. Includes emerging policy and legislation around AI systems.

title: Capstone: Business Models in Artificial Intelligence
description: Experiential capstone where student teams work with a company to develop AI models and strategy for a business domain. Covers problem definition, data curation, model training, and testing for accuracy, robustness, biases, and security risk.

title: AI, Migration Management & International Security
school: Rockefeller College of Public Affairs & Policy
description: Examines the intersection of artificial intelligence with migration management and international security. Covers AI-powered border systems, automated decision-making in immigration policy, and the ethical and governance implications of AI in state security contexts.

title: AI Plus Fundamentals
prereqs: None — open to all UAlbany undergraduates
description: A for-credit microcredential covering the technical fundamentals of artificial intelligence and its implications for modern society. Introduces AI terminology, machine learning, natural language processing, robotics, and image processing with hands-on activities.

title: Artificial Intelligence for Business
description: Equips students with expertise to harness AI for real-world business applications — streamlining operations, enhancing decision-making, and solving complex challenges across marketing, finance, human resources, cybersecurity, and accounting.

title: CGS Machine Learning
prereqs: Undergraduate background in mathematics, sciences, or social sciences. Mathematical deficiencies must be resolved before admission.
description: Certificate of Graduate Study providing a foundation in machine learning — the mathematical principles and practical skills to design algorithms that identify patterns and train machines to learn. Fully asynchronous online program. Credits may count toward the MS in Data Science.

title: CGS Geospatial Artificial Intelligence and Big Data Analytics
prereqs: Interest in using geographic data to support work in social sciences, environmental studies, market research, or urban planning.
description: Certificate training in GIS and spatial analysis using AI methods, statistics, automated cartography, remote sensing, and geographic visualization software. Prepares students for mapping projects in natural resource management, agriculture, environmental protection, and urban planning.

title: Google AI Essentials
description: Free, self-paced certificate introducing practical AI tools and responsible workflows for everyday productivity. Available free to all SUNY students, faculty, and staff through the SUNY–Google partnership.

title: Google Career Certificate: Data Analytics with AI
description: Free career certificate covering data analytics enhanced with AI-powered tools, designed for career readiness. Available free to all SUNY students, faculty, and staff through the SUNY–Google partnership.
